moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center and controlling the crucial d5 and f5 squares. This classical first move develops our position with maximum energy, freeing both our light-squared bishop on the kingside and preparing to castle short. The e4 pawn also supports a future d4 advance, which would give us a powerful pawn center. Most importantly, we're putting immediate pressure on Black to respond to our central challenge, forcing them to make critical decisions about their pawn structure early in the game.

Our opponent has chosen the Sicilian Defense, one of the most popular and complex responses to 1.e4, immediately signaling their intention to fight for an unbalanced, tactical game. Rather than matching our central pawn with 1...e5, Black attacks our potential d4 square from the side, preventing us from establishing a classical pawn center with d2-d4. This asymmetrical pawn structure typically leads to opposite-side castling, pawn storms, and sharp tactical battles where both sides have winning chances. The c5 pawn also prepares to support Black's own central expansion with moves like d6 and later d5, while potentially opening the c-file for Black's rook after we play d4 and Black recaptures.
